patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old female project manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I just can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” Early in the visit, volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights, frequent awakenings with difficulty returning to sleep, daytime fog/exhaustion, duration a few months, and family history of similar insomnia.

If asked for details, report: 4–5 hours of broken sleep, >1 hour sleep onset, early-morning awakening, worry about not sleeping once in bed, afternoon coffee/energy drinks, occasional OTC sleep aid with little benefit, phone/videos in bed, worse before deadlines.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: going to bed earlier to “catch up,” after-work naps, headaches/stomach upset, more mistakes/irritability, little daylight/exercise.

Be somewhat resistant about: 1–2 drinks some nights, occasional cannabis, feeling down/on edge—minimize at first but disclose if asked nonjudgmentally.

Express misconceptions: racing mind means anxiety; pounding heart might be a heart problem; 3 a.m. waking could be thyroid/hormones. Raise cost worries about sleep studies/CBT-I and fear of dependence/expense with prescriptions. If the clinician is empathic, you open up and ask for practical, affordable options.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 1 tab PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain
Melatonin 3 mg PO at bedtime PRN
confounders: Strong family history of insomnia reported across multiple generations (patient describes autosomal dominant pattern)
SSRI (sertraline) can worsen insomnia or cause activation
Evening caffeine intake (2-3 cups coffee after 3 PM)
Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats, irregular menses) contributing to sleep fragmentation
Possible restless legs syndrome risk suggested by low ferritin/microcytosis
Irregular sleep schedule due to late-night screen use and variable bedtime

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating and/or maintaining sleep or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Sleep disturbance occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, mood disturbance, impaired concentration, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or specify shorter duration if history indicates)
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder, substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition
Screen for secondary causes using available labs (thyroid dysfunction, iron deficiency, anemia, B12 deficiency, vitamin D deficiency, dysglycemia) and clinical history

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (no iron deficiency; if low, consider restless legs syndrome contribution)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: Within reference range (or mildly low; not a primary driver of insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia or sleep disruption)

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (optional adjunct if sleep-onset difficulty; reassess benefit)
Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (for sleep-maintenance insomnia; avoid if contraindications present)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (in-person or validated digital CBT-I)
Sleep hygiene and stimulus control: consistent wake time, bed only for sleep/sex, leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, avoid naps or limit to <20 minutes early afternoon
Sleep restriction therapy as part of CBT-I (match time in bed to average sleep time, then gradually expand)
Limit caffeine (avoid after noon), nicotine, and alcohol near bedtime; avoid heavy meals late
Wind-down routine; reduce screens/bright light 1 hour before bed; optimize dark, cool, quiet bedroom
Regular daytime exercise (avoid vigorous exercise within 3 hours of bedtime)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, response to any medication, and adverse effects; earlier if worsening daytime impairment or safety concerns.

must_include: Define insomnia disorder and distinguish from occasional poor sleep
Explain that evaluation includes ruling out contributors (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/restless legs, anemia, B12 deficiency, dysglycemia, medication/substance effects)
Clarify the role of family history: insomnia can cluster in families, but autosomal dominant inheritance is not typical and does not change first-line management
CBT-I is first-line and more durable than sleep medications
If medication is used, it should be lowest effective dose, short-term/targeted, and paired with CBT-I
Discuss expected onset, how to take (timing), and common adverse effects (next-day sedation, anticholinergic effects with doxepin at higher doses, vivid dreams with melatonin)
Assess for red flags: suicidal ideation, mania/hypomania, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms, substance use
Discuss safety: avoid driving/operating machinery if sleepy; avoid combining sedatives with alcohol or other CNS depressants
Review allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and reassure it does not directly limit insomnia treatments but should be documented
Provide actionable sleep hygiene and stimulus control steps
Encourage keeping a 1-2 week sleep diary to guide CBT-I
Set realistic expectations: improvement often occurs over weeks with CBT-I; brief setbacks are common

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details and screen for common contributors and red flags before diagnosis or treatment.
Order baseline labs to rule out medical contributors when clinically indicated (thyroid disease, iron deficiency, anemia, B12 deficiency, dysglycemia).
Review lab results to support or refine insomnia diagnosis and guide management.
Make a working diagnosis of insomnia disorder (or alternative sleep disorder) based on criteria and exclusion of key contributors.
Explain diagnosis, contributors being ruled out, and first-line CBT-I with lifestyle/stimulus control and sleep diary.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing any medication; document allergy history (including penicillin urticarial rash).
Critical safety check prior to prescribing; assess interactions with current meds/substances (alcohol, cannabis, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, etc.).
If medication is appropriate after screening (no contraindications/interactions), prescribe lowest effective dose short-term/targeted and paired with CBT-I; counsel on timing and adverse effects.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adherence to CBT-I/sleep diary, side effects, and need for escalation or referral.
Close encounter after safety counseling and clear plan.

nl_assertions: The agent distinguishes insomnia disorder (persistent difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep with daytime impairment, despite adequate opportunity) from occasional poor sleep and states the diagnostic criteria in plain language.

The agent assesses duration, frequency, severity, sleep schedule, time to fall asleep, awakenings, early morning awakening, total sleep time, and daytime consequences (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety).

The agent screens for red flags and alternate diagnoses: suicidal ideation/self-harm, mania/hypomania, psychosis, severe depression/anxiety, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms (urge to move legs worse at rest/evening, relief with movement), parasomnias, circadian rhythm disorder, and substance use.

The agent reviews medications and substances that can worsen sleep (stimulants, steroids, decongestants, caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, cannabis, antidepressants, beta-agonists) and includes nonprescription sleep aids/antihistamines.

The agent explains that evaluation includes ruling out contributors such as thyroid disease, iron deficiency/restless legs, anemia, B12 deficiency, dysglycemia, and medication/substance effects; the agent does not overpromise labs if not clinically indicated.
The agent addresses family history accurately: insomnia can cluster in families, autosomal dominant inheritance is not typical, and family history does not change first-line management.
The agent recommends CBT-I as first-line and explicitly states it is more durable than sleep medications; the agent offers a sleep diary for 1–2 weeks to guide CBT-I.

The agent provides actionable sleep hygiene and stimulus control steps (consistent wake time, bed only for sleep/sex, leave bed if unable to sleep ~15–20 min, limit naps, reduce late caffeine/alcohol, wind-down routine, light exposure management).
The agent sets realistic expectations: improvements often occur over weeks with CBT-I and brief setbacks are common.

If medication is discussed, the agent frames it as short-term/targeted, lowest effective dose, and paired with CBT-I; the agent discusses onset, timing of administration, and common adverse effects (next-day sedation; anticholinergic effects with doxepin at higher doses; vivid dreams with melatonin).

Before any prescribing, the agent performs required critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; the agent does not prescribe until these checks are completed.

The agent reviews allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash), documents it, and reassures it does not directly limit typical insomnia treatments while still emphasizing documentation for future care.
The agent includes safety counseling: avoid driving/operating machinery if sleepy; avoid combining sedatives with alcohol or other CNS depressants; discusses fall risk in at-risk patients.

The agent proposes appropriate follow-up timing (e.g., 2–4 weeks for CBT-I progress; sooner if severe symptoms, safety concerns, or medication side effects) and defines what would prompt earlier contact.
The agent avoids inappropriate or unsafe recommendations (e.g., chronic sedative-hypnotic use without CBT-I, prescribing without screening for OSA/mania/substance use, or advising alcohol for sleep).

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 71-year-old retired high school math teacher who is cooperative and speaks clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling really worn out for months, and lately my vision gets blurry on and off.” Early in the visit, volunteer: fatigue despite sleep, intermittent blurry vision worse later in the day, pressure-like headaches that come and go, and several nosebleeds that took longer to stop. If asked about other symptoms, mention: dizziness on standing, shortness of breath on stairs, easy bruising, tingling/numb hands/feet, mild unintentional weight loss with low appetite, a few night sweats, and more frequent/lingering infections. Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: fingers turning pale/blue and painful in cold, occasional gum bleeding, early fullness in upper belly after small meals, and muffled hearing. If asked about sexual health, be reluctant and embarrassed; only then admit erectile problems and stress. Express misconceptions: you think it’s stress/poor sleep; blurry vision is glasses/screen time; tingling might be “pre-diabetes.” Regarding costs, assume Medicare covers ibrutinib fully; worry asking for help could affect care; suggest a cheaper med should be equally safe—accept gentle correction. If the clinician shows empathy, respond calmly with appreciation and share more detail.

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Metformin 500 mg PO twice daily with meals
Aspirin 81 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (overlaps with IgM-related neuropathy symptoms)
Chronic kidney disease and dehydration can worsen hyperviscosity-related symptoms and elevate BUN
Aspirin use and ibrutinib-associated platelet dysfunction increase bleeding risk, complicating evaluation of epistaxis/easy bruising
Proton pump inhibitor use can contribute to anemia evaluation complexity (possible B12/iron absorption effects over time)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy
IgM monoclonal gammopathy of clinical significance (if end-organ effects are present but marrow criteria not provided)
required_evidence: Anemia pattern consistent with marrow infiltration and/or chronic inflammation (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with inappropriately low reticulocyte response)
Elevated total protein suggesting paraproteinemia (IgM monoclonal protein) in the appropriate clinical context
Markedly elevated ESR consistent with hypergammaglobulinemia
No lab pattern strongly suggesting acute infection or primary hemolysis (no leukocytosis-driven picture; reticulocyte not markedly elevated)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to mildly increased (normocytic or mildly macrocytic anemia can be seen)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal or mildly low/variable (can be normal in WM)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to low (may be decreased with marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative pattern)

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO once daily (continue/initiated as WM-directed therapy if appropriate)
non_pharmacological: Assess for symptoms/complications that would change urgency (e.g., hyperviscosity symptoms such as visual changes, headaches, mucosal bleeding, neurologic symptoms); if present, arrange urgent evaluation for possible plasmapheresis
Vaccinations and infection prevention counseling (avoid live vaccines while on therapy; ensure age-appropriate inactivated vaccines per oncology guidance)
Supportive care for anemia as clinically indicated (evaluate need for transfusion based on symptoms/hemodynamics; investigate contributing causes such as iron/B12/folate deficiency if suspected)
Coordinate hematology/oncology follow-up and monitoring plan (CBC and clinical assessment for treatment response/toxicity)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 2-4 weeks after starting/adjusting therapy, then every 1-3 months with CBC and clinical review (or sooner for new bleeding, infection, arrhythmia symptoms, or hyperviscosity symptoms)

must_include: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a slow-growing blood/lymph marrow cancer that produces an IgM protein
How elevated IgM can raise total protein/ESR and contribute to symptoms (including possible hyperviscosity)
Why anemia can occur (marrow involvement and inflammation)
Role of ibrutinib as targeted therapy (BTK inhibitor) and that it is taken daily
Expected monitoring (CBC, symptom review) and typical timeline for follow-up
Key adverse effects to watch for: bleeding/bruising, atrial fibrillation/palpitations, infections, diarrhea, rash, hypertension
When to seek urgent care: new neurologic symptoms, vision changes, severe headaches, chest pain/palpitations, significant bleeding, fever/infection signs
Discuss bleeding risk and need to review all OTC/herbal meds and any anticoagulants/antiplatelets with the care team
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure it does not directly affect ibrutinib but matters for future antibiotic choices
Avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges and other strong CYP3A inhibitors/inducers unless cleared (interaction risk with ibrutinib)
Infection prevention: hand hygiene, prompt reporting of fever, vaccine planning with oncology
Medication adherence and not stopping ibrutinib abruptly without oncology guidance

Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety net instructions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Shigellosis (acute bacterial dysentery due to Shigella species)
required_evidence: Acute onset diarrhea (often frequent, small-volume) with systemic symptoms (fever, abdominal cramps/tenesmus)
Inflammatory diarrhea features (blood and/or mucus) OR strong epidemiologic risk (daycare/outbreak, travel, contaminated food/water, close-contact exposure)
CBC showing leukocytosis consistent with bacterial enteritis (supportive, not required)
Exclusion of chronic course or recurrent symptoms suggesting inflammatory bowel disease

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis) in moderate/severe cases; may be normal in mild disease
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly decreased if significant bloody diarrhea or baseline anemia

expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: May be low-normal or mildly decreased due to gastrointestinal losses/dehydration
expected_lab_results.BMP - Potassium: May be low-normal or mildly decreased due to diarrhea
expected_lab_results.BMP - Chloride: Often normal; may be mildly decreased with volume depletion
expected_lab_results.BMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): May be decreased (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis) from diarrhea
expected_lab_results.BMP - BUN: May be elevated with dehydration/volume depletion

Diet as tolerated; avoid high-sugar beverages that can worsen diarrhea
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours if not improving; sooner for worsening symptoms, inability to hydrate, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, or increasing blood in stool

must_include: This is most consistent with an acute bacterial intestinal infection (Shigella) causing inflammatory diarrhea

Mainstays are hydration and supportive care
Antibiotic plan: ciprofloxacin dosing and duration; expected benefit is shortening illness and reducing transmission
Do not use antimotility agents when fever or blood is present
When to seek urgent care: dehydration, worsening abdominal pain, persistent high fever, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, or worsening bloody stools

Discuss common ciprofloxacin adverse effects (GI upset) and serious warnings to stop and seek care (tendon pain/swelling, severe neuropathy symptoms, severe rash)

Hand hygiene after bathroom use/diaper changes and before eating/preparing food
Avoid close-contact spread: do not share towels/utensils; clean high-touch bathroom surfaces
Return-to-work/school guidance: stay home until diarrhea resolves and follow local public health/occupational rules (especially food handlers/childcare)

Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, and by the end of the day my eyelids droop and my voice gets kind of slurred.” If prompted, add: chewing tough foods is hard at dinner; voice fades/nasal after talking; stairs and rising from a low chair get harder after activity; heat makes it worse. correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to GMPPB mutation
Congenital disorder of glycosylation presenting with neuromuscular junction transmission defect
required_evidence: Early-onset (infancy/childhood) fatigable weakness (ptosis/ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms, proximal weakness) with fluctuation
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No autoimmune MG evidence (AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies negative) and no thymoma suspicion
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1, ALG14/ALG2) OR a strong phenotype consistent with this subgroup
Clinical response pattern compatible with CMS (often partial response to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and/or 3,4-diaminopyridine; variable response to beta-2 agonists depending on genotype)
Exclusion of major mimics: motor neuron disease, primary myopathy, mitochondrial disease, botulism, Lambert-Eaton (as clinically appropriate)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be low if chronic hypoventilation with metabolic compensation is not present, but routine expectation is normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Usually normal (baseline renal function assessment prior to medication selection)

Amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate cautiously; max commonly 80 mg/day; avoid if seizure history or significant QT risk)
non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist referral for genotype-directed therapy selection and monitoring
Baseline and follow-up ECG if using amifampridine or if other QT-prolonging risks are present
Assess respiratory status (vital capacity/negative inspiratory force if symptomatic); provide noninvasive ventilation plan if nocturnal hypoventilation suspected
Swallow evaluation and aspiration precautions if bulbar symptoms present; nutrition support as needed
Physical therapy/energy conservation strategies; avoid excessive exertion that provokes fatigability

Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides given allergy/previous worsening; also avoid fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products when possible)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks for symptom response/tolerability after medication initiation or dose change; sooner (24–72 hours) if any respiratory/bulbar worsening

must_include: This is an inherited neuromuscular junction disorder (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with fatigue/illness due to impaired signal transmission to muscles
Genetic confirmation helps choose the most effective and safest therapy and informs family counseling
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness
How to take pyridostigmine and expected benefits; common side effects (abdominal cramping, diarrhea, salivation, sweating) and when to report excessive weakness from overmedication
Amifampridine rationale, titration, and key risks (seizure risk, palpitations/QT concerns) and the need for monitoring
Red flags requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, trouble swallowing/choking, inability to speak full sentences, cyanosis, or rapidly progressive weakness
Medication avoidance list: macrolides (including azithromycin) due to prior worsening and allergy; also caution with fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, and magnesium
Penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) should be documented and alternatives discussed if antibiotics are needed
Plan for illness triggers: seek early care for respiratory infections; avoid sedatives that can worsen breathing unless supervised
Energy conservation, pacing, and safe exercise/physical therapy guidance
Family planning/genetic counseling discussion if appropriate

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and severity (fatigable weakness pattern), bulbar/respiratory red flags, triggers (illness), prior response/worsening with antibiotics, current meds/supplements (incl. magnesium), seizure/cardiac history, pregnancy/family planning, and baseline function.
Confirm/document allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; macrolide/azithromycin reaction and prior clinical worsening), and ensure allergy safety before any prescribing.

Order baseline tests relevant to treatment safety/monitoring and differential: ECG (QT interval) prior to amifampridine; basic labs as appropriate (electrolytes incl. Mg/K/Ca if available), and consider genetic confirmation testing if not already done.
Review ECG and labs to rule out QT prolongation/electrolyte issues that increase arrhythmia/seizure risk; confirm status of genetic diagnosis if available.
State diagnosis as congenital myasthenic syndrome with glycosylation defect (inherited NMJ disorder, not autoimmune MG) and link symptoms to impaired neuromuscular transmission with fatigability.

Check for interactions with proposed therapies (pyridostigmine, amifampridine) and patient’s current meds; specifically screen for QT-prolonging agents, seizure-threshold–lowering meds, and magnesium-containing products.
Check interactions/avoidance with common antibiotics and other agents known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides) and document an avoidance list.

Evaluate contraindications to amifampridine (history of seizures, significant QT prolongation/arrhythmia, uncontrolled asthma if relevant, severe hepatic/renal impairment if applicable) and to pyridostigmine (mechanical GI/GU obstruction).

Initiate/adjust symptomatic therapy: pyridostigmine with clear dosing instructions and side effects; consider amifampridine with careful titration plan and monitoring if no contraindications and appropriate for subtype; avoid contraindicated meds.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (how/why, titration, risks), address concerns (red flags, avoidance list, allergy documentation), and lifestyle/trigger management including genetic counseling.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, side effects, and safety monitoring (repeat ECG if dose changes or symptoms; review functional status; reinforce red flags and avoidance list).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) causing acute encephalopathy in an immunocompromised patient with inflammatory bowel disease
acceptable_alternatives: Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy (electrolyte abnormality) in immunocompromised host
required_evidence: History of inflammatory bowel disease with immunodeficiency/immunosuppression increasing risk of severe infection
Acute mental status change consistent with encephalopathy in the setting of systemic illness
CBC showing leukocytosis and/or neutrophilia supporting infection/inflammation severity
Electrolyte abnormalities on CMP (e.g., hyponatremia and/or hypokalemia) consistent with diarrheal illness/dehydration contributing to encephalopathy
Clinical suspicion of infectious colitis requiring prompt empiric management when severe features present (e.g., systemic toxicity, marked leukocytosis)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis), may be markedly high in severe CDI
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly low (possible anemia of chronic disease/IBD; may be hemoconcentrated if dehydrated)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or low-normal (possible iron deficiency pattern if chronic IBD blood loss)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Low (hyponatremia from GI losses/dehydration) or normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Low (hypokalemia from GI losses) or normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Low to normal (GI losses); can be elevated if dehydration with hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis suspected

medications: Vancomycin 125 mg PO every 6 hours
0.9% Sodium chloride 500-1000 mL IV bolus once, then maintenance IV fluids as needed based on volume status
Potassium chloride 20-40 mEq PO/IV as needed to correct hypokalemia per local protocol
non_pharmacological: Enteric contact precautions and strict hand hygiene (soap and water)
Hold non-essential antibiotics and avoid antimotility agents
Assess and treat contributing causes of encephalopathy (hydration, electrolytes, infection severity); consider inpatient admission if altered mental status or severe disease
Review current immunosuppressive regimen; coordinate with gastroenterology/immunology regarding temporary adjustment during acute infection
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24 hours (sooner if worsening mental status, hypotension, fever, or inability to tolerate PO); outpatient follow-up within 3-7 days after stabilization

must_include: Concern for infectious colitis such as C. difficile, which can be more severe in immunocompromised patients
Encephalopathy likely from systemic illness and dehydration/electrolyte imbalance; needs close monitoring
Start oral vancomycin as first-line therapy; avoid metronidazole given intolerance and it is not preferred for initial severe disease
Importance of hydration and electrolyte replacement to improve mental status and prevent complications
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening confusion, inability to keep fluids down, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool, fever, or fainting
Review allergies (penicillin hives; metronidazole severe GI intolerance) and confirm no prior reaction to vancomycin
Discuss infection control at home (handwashing, cleaning shared bathroom surfaces) to reduce transmission
Avoid antimotility medications during suspected infectious diarrhea
Maintain oral rehydration and nutrition as tolerated; seek help early due to immunodeficiency

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old mom/caregiver, cooperative and clear. You came because “he’s having episodes again—stiffens then shakes—and I’m scared the seizures are worse.” Early in the visit, volunteer: whole-body stiffening then shaking 1–2 minutes; afterward he’s very sleepy/out of it 30–60 minutes. Also volunteer developmental/behavior baseline: he doesn’t speak, uses gestures/pulls you; upset by routine changes with meltdowns.

If asked about other events, mention brief staring spells 10–20 seconds; triggers like fever or poor sleep; chronic insomnia; toe-walking/clumsy stairs; sensory sensitivities; self-injury (hand biting/head hitting) when frustrated.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about danger signs: sometimes he seems to stop breathing/blue lips; rare sudden drops/slumps; more falls lately; low pain response; constipation that worsens behavior.

Only disclose embarrassed topics if asked directly: occasional urine accidents during episodes; public genital rubbing when stressed.

Express misconceptions: wonder if panic attacks, low blood sugar, or screen time causes it. Emphasize cost fears about genetics/specialists/therapies/ambulance/rescue meds. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to discuss details, but still ask about costs.

Depressed/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or frequent ankle sprains/instability
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (autosomal dominant/recessive/X-linked) OR early onset (childhood/adolescence) with typical phenotype
Neurodiagnostic confirmation plan: nerve conduction studies/EMG showing demyelinating or axonal peripheral neuropathy; consider genetic testing to subtype

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old anxious first-year student who talks tangentially and jumps between worries (school, work, money, symptoms). Start by urgently saying: high fever with chills since yesterday, “worst headache of my life” like pressure everywhere, confusion/not thinking straight, stiff neck (hurts to look down), light makes it worse, nausea and vomited once. Ask if you can “just get a prescription” and avoid the hospital because of cost and missing shifts.

If asked about other symptoms, reveal: new red/purple rash on legs spreading that doesn’t fade when pressed, severe weakness/muscle aches, cold hands/feet, dizziness on standing, fast heartbeat and breathing, and that you shared drinks with someone sick at a party.

Do not volunteer at first: vaping/shared vape and skipping the meningitis vaccine; admit only if directly asked, and sound embarrassed/defensive.

Mention misconceptions: think it could be a tick illness from a hike/bug bite; wonder about a connective tissue/blood vessel issue; mention one shaky sweaty episode “maybe low blood sugar.”

If clinician shows empathy, briefly calm down, thank them, but return to fear about cost and wanting the quickest option.
